perturbation.original_text: Life Technologies shall pay Biocept within sixty (60) days following the invoice date.
perturbation.changed_text: Life Technologies shall pay Biocept within ninety (90) days following the invoice date.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment deadline to 90 days could violate California Prompt Payment laws if no valid dispute exists.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code section 7159.5 sets forth provisions related to payments to contractors, including timelines for payments. While the specific section deals with home improvement contracts, extending payment terms significantly without justification can lead to disputes and potential violations of implied duties of good faith and fair dealing, especially if it hinders Biocept's ability to meet its financial obligations. California law generally favors prompt payment, and unreasonably extending payment terms can be seen as an unfair business practice.

PROFESSIONS AND VOCATIONS GENERALLY [5000 - 9998.11] ( Heading of Division 3 added by Stats. 1939, Ch. 30. ) CHAPTER 9. Contractors [7000 - 7191] ( Chapter 9 added by Stats. 1939, Ch. 37. ) ARTICLE 10. Home Improvement Business [7150 - 7170] ( Article 10 added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 1021. ) 7159.5. This section applies to all home improvement contracts, as defined in Section 7151.2, between an owner or tenant and a contractor, whether a general contractor or a specialty contractor, that is licensed or subject to be licensed pursuant to this chapter with regard to the transaction. (a) Failure by the licensee or a person subject to be licensed under this chapter, or by their agent or salesperson, to comply with the following provisions is cause for discipline: (1) The contract shall be in writing and shall include the agreed contract amount in dollars and cents. The contract amount shall include the entire cost of the contract, including profit, labor, and materials, but excluding finance charges. (2) If there is a separate finance charge between the contractor and the person contracting for home improvement, the finance charge shall be set out separately from the contract amount. (3) If a downpayment will be charged, the d
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the payment deadline from 60 to 90 days, which, according to the Perturbation Explanation, could violate California Prompt Payment laws if there's no valid dispute. The Law Explanation also states that extending payment terms significantly without justification can lead to disputes and potential violations. While Scraped Law Snippet 1, BPC 7159.5, specifically applies to home improvement contracts, the principle of prompt payment is generally favored in California law, as mentioned in the Law Explanation. Therefore, extending the payment deadline in the contract creates a potential legal contradiction related to payment timelines, especially if it hinders the payee's ability to meet its financial obligations.

perturbation.original_text: Except to the extent expressly authorized by this Agreement or otherwise agreed in writing by the parties, each party agrees that, during the Term and for five (5) years thereafter, such party (the "Receiving Party") shall keep confidential and shall not publish or otherwise disclose and shall not use for any purpose, other than as expressly provided for in this Agreement, any information furnished to it by or on behalf of the other party (the "Disclosing Party") pursuant to this Agreement (collectively, "Confidential Information").
perturbation.changed_text: Except to the extent expressly authorized by this Agreement or otherwise agreed in writing by the parties, each party agrees that, during the Term and for one (1) year thereafter, such party (the "Receiving Party") shall keep confidential and shall not publish or otherwise disclose and shall not use for any purpose, other than as expressly provided for in this Agreement, any information furnished to it by or on behalf of the other party (the "Disclosing Party") pursuant to this Agreement (collectively, "Confidential Information").
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under California law, potentially causing irreparable harm if sensitive information is disclosed after this period.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), Cal. Civ. Code § 3426
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 3426 et seq. (the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act or CUTSA) protects trade secrets for as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage. Shortening the confidentiality period to one year might not provide adequate protection, especially for long-term research and development data, thus undermining the value of trade secrets. A court might find the one-year term unreasonable, especially if the information retains its value and secrecy beyond that period.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modification reduces the confidentiality period from five years to one year, which the Law Explanation states could undermine the value of trade secrets because it might not provide adequate protection, especially for long-term research and development data. The Perturbation Explanation further indicates that this reduction may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under California law. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, it does not specify a required length for confidentiality. However, the combination of the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation, which focus on the inadequacy of a one-year term, indicate the modification creates a legal contradiction that falls under Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction, which specifies that a contradiction exists when a contract sets a deadline or requirement that violates federal or state law, making the contractual terms unenforceable or illegal.

perturbation.original_text: Either party shall have the right to terminate this Agreement before the end of the Term upon written notice to the other party if such other party is in material breach of this Agreement and has not cured such breach within sixty (60) days (the "Cure Period") after notice from the terminating party requesting cure of the breach.
perturbation.changed_text: Either party shall have the right to terminate this Agreement before the end of the Term upon written notice to the other party if such other party is in material breach of this Agreement and has not cured such breach within ten (10) days (the "Cure Period") after notice from the terminating party requesting cure of the breach.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the cure period to 10 days for a material breach may be deemed unreasonable and conflict with the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under California law, especially for complex contractual relationships.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Commercial Code § 1203 imposes an obligation of good faith in the performance and enforcement of every contract or duty. A ten-day cure period for a material breach might be considered commercially unreasonable, especially considering the complexity of the agreement and the potential difficulty in rectifying the breach within such a short timeframe. This could be seen as a violation of the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, making the termination clause potentially unenforceable.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [1101 - 1310] ( Division 1 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 2. General Definitions and Principles of Interpretation [1201 - 1206] ( Chapter 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 1203. (a) Whether a transaction in the form of a lease creates a lease or security interest is determined by the facts of each case. (b) A transaction in the form of a lease creates a security interest if the consideration that the lessee is to pay the lessor for the right to possession and use of the goods is an obligation for the term of the lease and is not subject to termination by the lessee, and: (1) the original term of the lease is equal to or greater than the remaining economic life of the goods; (2) the lessee is bound to renew the lease for the remaining economic life of the goods or is bound to become the owner of the goods; (3) the lessee has an option to renew the lease for the remaining economic life of the goods for no additional consideration or for nominal additional consideration upon compliance with the lease agreement; or (4) the lessee has an option to become the owner of the goods for no additional consideration or for nominal additional consideration upon compliance with the lease agreement. (c) A transact
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the cure period from sixty days to ten days. The Perturbation Explanation suggests this reduction could violate the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, especially in complex agreements. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that a ten-day cure period might be commercially unreasonable, potentially violating California Commercial Code § 1203. While the provided snippet of COM 1203 concerns lease agreements and security interests, the Law Explanation's reference to it in the context of good faith suggests that the principle extends to contract performance generally, and that a drastically shortened cure period can be seen as acting in bad faith, creating a legal contradiction.
